summary: On September 11th, 2001, I was off duty and happened to be in Williamsburg, Virginia with my wife. We were stunned and devastated as we watched the news unfold about the hijacked airlines crashing into public buildings. As a law enforcement supervisor, I immediately thought about how this would impact security in the National Park Service, especially in our parks. Within weeks, we began sending rangers to high-risk parks for homeland security assignments, which has continued since then.

I was in Williamsburg, Virginia on my day off with my wife when the September 11 attacks happened. We were out shopping and recreating in the area when we first heard the news. We quickly returned to our unit to watch the television coverage and absorb the shocking events. Williamsburg was where I first learned about the attacks before heading back to work at Shenandoah National Park.

I serve as the District Ranger for the South District of Shenandoah National Park, which covers about 81,000 acres. After the September 11 attacks, I had to manage how our law enforcement rangers were detailed to homeland security assignments across the country while maintaining coverage here. Although security concerns at Shenandoah were minimal, we had to adjust patrols and schedules to accommodate those assignments. We focused more on developed areas like Skyland and Big Meadows, while the park's extreme ends saw less frequent patrols.

As a supervisor within the National Park Service, the September 11 attacks prompted immediate questions about how we would protect parks nationwide. We quickly identified high-risk parks and began sending law enforcement rangers on homeland security details. This required careful planning to maintain essential park functions despite reduced staffing. The National Park Service's security efforts have been ongoing since then, adapting to new threats and assignments.

After September 11, many of our rangers were detailed to Independence National Historical Park in Philadelphia as it was considered a high-risk target. Early homeland security assignments focused heavily on this park along with Boston National Historical Park. Our personnel rotated through these assignments, often working long shifts to help bolster security at these critical historical sites.

Boston National Historical Park was one of the primary locations where our rangers were sent for homeland security details after the September 11 attacks. It was identified as a high-risk park on the East Coast, requiring enhanced security presence. Rangers from Shenandoah and other parks rotated through these assignments, often working 12 to 14 hour days for 21-day tours. This was a significant effort to protect important historical sites in the aftermath of the attacks.

In the months following September 11, a few of our rangers were assigned short-term homeland security details at the Interior Building in Washington, D.C. While most assignments were at parks like Independence and Boston, the Interior Building was also recognized as a location needing additional security. These assignments were part of the broader effort coordinated by the National Park Service and related agencies.

Washington, D.C. was a focal point for homeland security efforts after September 11, with some of our rangers detailed to the Interior Building there. The city’s importance as the nation’s capital made it a high-risk area requiring additional law enforcement presence. Coordination for these assignments was managed through the Eastern Interagency Communications Center located nearby in Shenandoah National Park.

In recent months after the initial homeland security efforts, some of our rangers have been detailed to Shasta National Recreation Area in California. This was part of a broader expansion of security assistance beyond the initial Eastern parks. Shasta, along with places like Hoover Dam, required additional support from parks like ours to maintain security in the post-9/11 environment.

Hoover Dam, near Lake Mead National Recreation Area, became one of the locations where our rangers were sent for homeland security details after the initial focus on Eastern parks. This assignment reflected the growing recognition of critical infrastructure sites needing protection. Rangers from Shenandoah and other parks helped provide security there as part of the ongoing national effort.

Lake Mead National Recreation Area, near Hoover Dam, was another site where homeland security assignments took place. Our rangers were sent there to assist with security following the September 11 attacks. This was part of a broader strategy to protect high-risk areas across the country, including major recreation and infrastructure sites.

The Eastern Interagency Communications Center, located within Shenandoah National Park, played a crucial role in coordinating homeland security assignments after September 11. They managed the mobilization, scheduling, and deployment of rangers to various high-risk parks and locations. Their efficient planning allowed us to quickly adjust staffing and maintain essential park functions despite personnel being detailed elsewhere.

Skyland is one of the two lodges in Shenandoah National Park where we focused our patrol and emergency response efforts after September 11. Because visitors stay there every night, it became a priority area for maintaining rapid law enforcement presence. With reduced staffing due to homeland security assignments, we concentrated coverage around Skyland to ensure visitor safety.

Big Meadows, like Skyland, is a key developed area within Shenandoah National Park where we maintained focused patrol coverage after September 11. Given the steady visitor activity there, it was important to ensure quick emergency response despite reduced manpower. Our rangers were scheduled to prioritize these areas to balance homeland security assignments with visitor safety.

I spoke with rangers who were working out at Manassas, and they described their homeland security assignments as very demanding, similar to being on a big fire project out West. These assignments involved long shifts and left them quite tired upon return. Manassas was one of the locations where our personnel were detailed to help with increased security following September 11.
